text: The surgeon aligns the transected ends of the small bowel and colon after mobilizing the small bowel mesentery. A stay suture is placed on the anti-mesenteric border of both the colon and small bowel, held by an assistant using a suprapubic port. The surgeon uses Maryland forceps to create a small enterotomy in the bowel and a corresponding colotomy in the colon, both on the anti-mesenteric side.

The surgeon introduces the stapler, loading the cartridge end into the small bowel. Using the stay suture and graspers, the anvil end is maneuvered into the colon, ensuring the small bowel and colon limbs are of equal length.

The surgeon fires the stapler to create a common channel for the ileocolic anastomosis, with no visible hemorrhage. The first stitch is placed through the staple line, starting on the colon side and passing full thickness through the small bowel, with the knot tied down on the staple line. Subsequent stitches replicate this technique, with the needle loaded and positioned for each stitch. The left hand retracts and orients the bowel for suturing, cinching down the running stitch with the reloaded needle. This process is repeated to close the entire anastomosis.

The surgeon places stitches closely together to bring the bowel ends back together, running the suture up to the stay suture placed at the beginning. The stitches are tied down, with the stay suture positioned away from the apex of the incision in the bowel and colon.

The surgeon completes the final throws of the stitch, finishing the ileocolic anastomosis in one layer. The bowel is oriented to ensure correct alignment of the bowel ends and mesentery, confirming there is no twist. A second layer of the anastomosis is performed, resulting in a complete anastomosis. The colon specimen is extracted using a fan and steel technique.
